topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crumpled envelope lay on the kitchen counter, a stark white rectangle mocking me. My hands were shaking as I picked it up, the paper feeling brittle under my grip. "Moving," the words swam in my head, sharp and unwelcome. I stared at the address, the unfamiliar city name a physical jolt. I wanted to rip the paper in half, to make a noise that matched the tightness coiling in my chest. How could they just *do* this? After everything we’d built? I slammed the envelope down, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet apartment. I walked to the window, the city lights blurring into meaningless streaks. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. This felt like a betrayal, a deliberate severing of something vital.

[story 2]

I read the text message again, the screen illuminating my face in the dim light of my room. The sheer audacity of it hit me like a physical blow. Across the country. It wasn't a casual decision; it was a definitive exit. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat, something rough and uncontrolled. I shoved my phone onto the bedside table with unnecessary force, the plastic protesting the sudden movement. My breath came in short, shallow gasps, each one laced with a burning sensation behind my eyes. I paced the small space, my movements jerky and sharp. Every object in the room seemed to vibrate with the tension radiating off me. I needed to scream, to dismantle the news, but the words caught in my throat, leaving only a hollow, vibrating emptiness where sound should have been.

[story 3]

When I saw the confirmation email, the color drained from my skin. It wasn't sadness I felt; it was a hot, prickly residue spreading through my limbs. I stood stock-still by the doorway, my knuckles white where I gripped the frame. The air in the hallway felt thick, suffocating. My vision narrowed, focusing only on the stark, undeniable facts of the situation. I took a deep, ragged breath, trying to steady the frantic drumming beneath my ribs. I walked back to my desk, my steps heavy and deliberate, each footfall a declaration of internal upheaval. I picked up a pen, but instead of writing, I just ground it between my fingers until the plastic warped slightly under the pressure. The silence of the room amplified the furious churning inside me.
